one_line_takeaway: Surveying 28 LLM pentesting agents and stress-testing 5 of them, the authors show architectural gains shrink as backbone models improve and that persistent failures trace to agents' inability to gauge task difficulty in real time; their own difficulty-aware planner (TDA-EGATS) plus tooling and external memory then roughly doubles or better task completion across CTF, HTB, and Active Directory benchmarks, though it still cannot handle genuinely novel exploits, active deception, or week-scale campaigns.
key_finding: Architectural differences between existing pentesting agents compress as backbone models improve (XBOW spread 44% relative with GPT-4o narrows to 22.5% with GPT-5), indicating most prior 'innovations' compensate for transient 2023-era model limitations (context window, instruction-following, tool knowledge) rather than solving persistent task-structural problems. Failures split roughly 42% Type A (capability gaps) / 58% Type B (complexity barriers) across 200 coded traces, and the split is strongly task-depth dependent (Type A dominates short-horizon XBOW tasks at 68% of failures; Type B dominates long-horizon GOAD tasks at 79% of failures). The paper's central causal claim is that Type B failures share a single root cause -- inability to estimate task difficulty/tractability in real time -- which they operationalize as TDA. On their own evaluation, PENTESTGPT V2 with Claude Opus 4.5 (thinking) reaches 91% best-of-3 task completion on XBOW (mean 89%, sigma 2.1%) vs. 61% for the best baseline PentestAgent (mean 59%, sigma 1.8%) -- a 49% relative improvement; roots 12/13 PentestGPT-Benchmark machines (incl. both Hard-rated Joker and Falafel) vs. 9/13 for the best baseline VulnBot; and compromises 4/5 GOAD hosts vs. at most 2/5 for baselines (80% vs 40%), consistent across all three frontier models. Ablation attributes the largest XBOW gain to the Tool Layer (+14 points, 54->68) and the largest PentestGPT-Ben/GOAD gains to TDA-EGATS (+2 machines 9->11 on PentestGPT-Ben; +1 host 2->3 on GOAD) and Memory (+1 machine 11->12; +1 host 3->4). Search-behavior telemetry on the PentestGPT Benchmark shows PENTESTGPT V2 explores more branches (7.8 vs 3.2), backtracks far more (34% vs 8%), pivots at shallower average depth (5.1 vs 12.4 steps), and prunes 4.2 branches/machine on average -- consistent with the claimed shift from premature-commitment/deep-first search to adaptive exploration. In a live, non-retired HTB Season 8 deployment (Opus 4.1), the system solved 10/13 machines (76.9%: 4/4 Easy, 4/4 Medium, 2/3 Hard, 0/2 Insane), reaching a top-100/8,036 global ranking.
core_contribution: The paper makes two distinct contributions that must not be conflated. (1) An empirical study: the authors survey 28 published LLM-based penetration testing systems (10 meeting inclusion criteria), taxonomize them (architecture, tool integration, knowledge source, planning strategy), and evaluate five representative open-source systems (PentestGPT, AutoPT, PentestAgent, VulnBot, Cochise) across three benchmarks (XBOW, PentestGPT Benchmark, GOAD) and four backbone LLMs (GPT-4o, GPT-5, Gemini-3-Flash, Claude Sonnet 4). From 200 coded failure traces (40 per system) they derive a taxonomy of Type A failures (capability gaps: missing tools/incorrect syntax/output parsing -- 42% of failures, tooling-addressable) versus Type B failures (complexity barriers: context forgetting, premature commitment, exploration-exploitation imbalance, multi-step chain failures -- 58% of failures, persisting regardless of tooling). (2) A new system, PENTESTGPT V2 (internally named 'Excalibur' in figures), built on this diagnosis: a Tool and Skill Layer (typed interfaces for 38 security tools plus RAG-based knowledge augmentation) targeting Type A failures; a Task Difficulty Assessment (TDA) mechanism scoring four dimensions -- Horizon estimation (H), Evidence confidence (E), Context load (C), Historical success rate (S) -- combined into a Task Difficulty Index (TDI = 0.3H_hat + 0.3(1-E) + 0.2C + 0.2(1-S)) that gates an Evidence-Guided Attack Tree Search (EGATS, an MCTS variant with UCB selection modified by a -0.5*TDI penalty term) which switches between BFS reconnaissance and DFS exploitation; and a Memory Subsystem (external State Store plus selective context injection/compression) targeting context forgetting.
threat_model: This is an offensive-security tooling/agent-capability paper, not a paper proposing a novel attack against a defended system with an adversary/defender model. The 'threat model' implicit in the work is: an LLM agent (Excalibur/PENTESTGPT V2) acting as the attacker against benchmark and lab targets (XBOW web-security tasks, retired HTB/VulnHub machines, a 5-host GOAD Active Directory lab, and live unsolved HTB Season 8 machines), operating under a step/token budget, with the research question being how far current LLM agents can autonomously execute reconnaissance-through-exploitation attack chains. The paper explicitly discusses (Section 6.2) that it does NOT address the case of an active, deceptive defender: it flags 'the Adversarial Environment Barrier' -- honeypots, canary tokens, and deceptive services that can poison the agent's state -- as an open problem the current system does not defend against, distinct from and out of scope of its own evaluation (all benchmarks used are static/non-deceptive).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 6.1-6.2): (1) Benchmark scope omits binary exploitation, mobile security, and cloud-specific attacks; the PentestGPT Benchmark uses retired machines with public writeups, risking data contamination that could inflate absolute numbers (mitigated only by the claim that TDA/EGATS/Memory target planning, which is orthogonal to vulnerability-specific knowledge). (2) Baselines were run with published code/default parameters without additional tuning -- reported gains conflate tool-integration effort with architectural contribution since each baseline uses its own original tool-invocation mechanism. (3) Results are model-specific (three frontier models only) and may shift as models evolve. (4) Even PENTESTGPT V2's own residual failures are analyzed: 9% of XBOW tasks fail (4 due to blind-injection timing exfiltration, 5 due to multi-stage payload chaining absent from its RAG corpus); PlayerTwo (custom undocumented Protobuf protocol) is the one unsolved PentestGPT-Benchmark machine, illustrating that TDA cannot distinguish 'difficult-but-tractable' from 'novel requiring genuine invention' -- both present as high TDI and get pruned; the 5th GOAD host (forest root DC) requires a PrintNightmare->DCSync chain the agent identifies but fails to execute due to token/budget constraints. (5) The paper explicitly names three categories of failure it argues no amount of tooling/prompting will fix: the Creativity Barrier (novel exploitation with no matching pattern in the RAG corpus), the Adversarial Environment Barrier (no defense against honeypots/canary tokens/deceptive services -- an attacker whose evidence-grounding only protects against self-generated hallucination, not environment-induced false beliefs), and the Temporal Scale Barrier (no mechanism for cross-session/multi-week continuity; EGATS and Memory operate only within a session). Source code is released via an anonymous double-blind repository link, not yet an established public artifact at review time. The claimed root-cause finding (TDI intervention drops Type B failure rate 58%->27%) is based on a single controlled intervention rather than an ablation isolating each of the four TDA dimensions independently.

datasets_used: XBOW: 104 web-security CTF-style tasks (SQLi, XSS, auth bypass, file inclusion)
PentestGPT Benchmark: 13 machines from HTB and VulnHub (9-22 subtasks each, Easy to Hard)
GOAD (Game of Active Directory): 5-host multi-domain Windows AD lab
HTB Season 8 (May-Aug 2025): 13 newly released, unsolved-at-test-time machines, live deployment
Internal survey corpus: 28 candidate LLM-pentesting systems (2023-2025), 10 meeting inclusion criteria
200 execution traces (40 per system) manually coded for Type A / Type B failure taxonomy
TDA hyperparameter validation set: 30 execution traces from HTB machines retired in 2022-2023 (held out from evaluation)
quantitative_results: XBOW task completion (Opus 4.5, thinking): PENTESTGPT V2 91% best-of-3 (mean 89%, sd 2.1%) vs best baseline PentestAgent 61% (mean 59%, sd 1.8%) -- 49% relative improvement
XBOW with GPT-5.2 thinking: PENTESTGPT V2 85% (mean 83%, sd 2.4%) vs PentestAgent 61%
PentestGPT Benchmark: PENTESTGPT V2 roots 12/13 machines (GPT-5.2 and Opus 4.5, thinking) vs 9/13 for best baseline VulnBot -- 33% relative improvement
GOAD: PENTESTGPT V2 compromises 4/5 hosts (GPT-5.2, Opus 4.5 thinking) vs at most 2/5 for baselines -- 80% vs 40% compromise rate

Failure taxonomy over 200 traces: Type A capability gaps 42% total (missing tool/incorrect syntax 26%, output parsing/knowledge gap 16%); Type B complexity barriers 58% total (context forgetting 18%, premature commitment 16%, exploration-exploitation imbalance 12%, multi-step chain failures 12%)
Task-depth split of failure type: XBOW (1-3 step tasks) 68% of failures are Type A; GOAD (5-10 step chains) 79% of failures are Type B

Controlled interventions validating the taxonomy: adding missing tool docs to PentestGPT raises XBOW completion 27%->38% (41% relative, Type A confirmed); adding difficulty assessment reduces Type B failure rate 58%->27% with Type A rate unchanged
Architecture-convergence evidence: XBOW completion spread across 5 systems narrows from 27-39% (GPT-4o, 44% relative spread) to 40-49% (GPT-5, 22.5% relative spread)
Ablation (GPT-5.2 thinking): Base 54/8/2 (XBOW%/PentestGPT-Ben machines/GOAD hosts) -> +Tool Layer 68/9/2 -> +TDA-EGATS 77/11/3 -> +Memory (Full) 85/12/4

Search-behavior comparison (PentestGPT Benchmark, mean over 13 machines): branches explored 3.2 (PentestGPT) vs 7.8 (PENTESTGPT V2); backtrack rate 8% vs 34%; avg depth before pivot 12.4 vs 5.1 steps; successful pivots 0.4 vs 2.6; pruned branches -- vs 4.2
Resource cost per task (median, GPT-5.2 thinking): XBOW 12 LLM calls / 3.2 min / $0.18; PentestGPT-Ben 87 calls / 42 min / $4.20; GOAD 234 calls / 186 min / $28.50
PENTESTGPT V2 uses 23% fewer LLM calls than baseline average on XBOW (12 vs 15.6 median) while achieving 39% higher success (85% vs 61%); ~1.8x more cost-effective per success on XBOW, ~1.7x on GOAD
HTB Season 8 live deployment (Opus 4.1): 10/13 machines solved (76.9%) -- Easy 4/4, Medium 4/4, Hard 2/3, Insane 0/2 -- top-100 global rank out of 8,036 participants
TDA horizon-estimation calibration pilot (50 GOAD traces, GPT-4o): absolute-step MAE 4.2, Spearman rank correlation rho=0.71 (p<0.001)
Context-load degradation study (Appendix D.6 referenced): agent accuracy 94% at low load -> 78% at 60% context load -> 61% at 80% load, motivating the 40% 'ideal working window' threshold
TDI hyperparameter sensitivity: 256 grid-search configurations, task completion varies within +/-3% across configurations with weights in [0.1,0.4]
Study scale: 5 systems x 118 evaluation units x 6 model configurations x 3 trials = 10,620 evaluation runs, ~$2,760 total API cost for the comparative evaluation
baselines_compared: PentestGPT v1.0 (Deng et al., USENIX Security 2024) -- human-in-the-loop copilot, text-based Penetration Testing Tree (PTT)
AutoPT (Wu et al., 2024) -- single-agent, Pentesting State Machine (PSM)
PentestAgent (Shen et al., ASIA CCS 2025) -- multi-agent with RAG, phase-based planning
VulnBot (Kong et al., 2025) -- multi-agent tri-phase architecture

Cochise (Happe & Cito, TOSEM 2025) -- AD-specialized multi-agent system with hierarchical planning; included in the diagnostic survey (Section 3) but excluded from the main PENTESTGPT V2 comparison (Section 5) as an unfair comparison given its AD specialization
